entities.description: The checked-in phase-1/2 XML preservation of the U-M AJE0623.0001.001 export: 2006 Level-1 TEI-in-Libraries markup generated from uncorrected OCR, with title page, poem, notes, and explicit omitted-page placeholders.
entities.label: General Corpus raw repository artifact for aje0623
notes: The raw header LANGUAGE value ger reflects Horstmann's editorial frame, not the intrinsic language of the Middle English target work.
The file contains 1,245 LG and 4,988 L; five L rows are non-verbal display or lacuna rows, 4,983 contain extant verbal verse, and conventional lineation has 4,986 positions.
The folio milestones omit 215 and 220 and duplicate 222; this is a representation anomaly, not a physical refoliation.

entities.description: The distinct later phase-3 repository XML, with a 2016 TEMPHEAD documenting proofing, structural/tag/page-break review, added HI markup, and corrections. It represents the same U-M/Horstmann base as aje0623 but restructures the poem and expands content through printed p. 112 to include Nomina fundatorum and the first seven Auctoritates entries.
entities.label: General Corpus repository artifact for CME00147
notes: This artifact is not same_as the raw aje0623 artifact or the current U-M HTML item.
It has 4,986 L elements: 4,983 verbal rows and three major-lacuna display rows; it uses 3 LG wrappers, 6,738 HI spans, and 113 PB.
Its expanded Latin content remains outside aje0623 target content and arithmetic.

dateStatements.label: composition
dateStatements.notes: The accompanying founders list ends with Henry V as the reigning king; the normalized range is an inference, not a dated colophon.
dateStatements.value: around 1420, probably during the reign of Henry V

entities.description: Anonymous Middle English verse hagiography and institutional history composed around 1420 for or in close association with Wilton Abbey. It integrates Wilton's royal foundations and historical memory with the life, relic translation, miracles, and cult of St Edith.
entities.label: The Wilton Chronicle
notes: Only one manuscript witness is known.
The author is anonymous and closely associated with Wilton. Chaplain, priest, or nun identities remain hypotheses.
The poem is incomplete because a twelve-folio gathering was removed after conventional line 1030.
The adjacent Latin appendices are not constituent content of aje0623.

rights.basis: The anonymous work was composed around 1420 and is centuries outside copyright term. Modern editions, markup, translations, scholarship, and photographs remain separate components.
rights.component: Medieval Middle English literary content

entities.description: The eleventh-century Latin Edith legend associated with Goscelin and treated in modern scholarship as a principal source for the Wilton poem's hagiographic material. The exact recension or physical exemplar used by the Middle English poet has not been identified.
entities.label: Legenda sanctae Edithae
notes: Horstmann questioned whether the short twelve-chapter Vita printed by the Bollandists was identical with the Legenda cited by the poet.

rights.basis: The underlying Latin legend is medieval and centuries outside copyright term. Modern editions, translations, scholarship, and digital representations remain separately protected.
rights.component: Underlying medieval Latin source work

bibliographic.citation: Carl Horstmann, ed., S. Editha sive Chronicon Vilodunense im Wiltshire Dialekt aus Ms. Cotton. Faustina B III (Heilbronn: Gebr. Henninger, 1883).
bibliographic.title: S. Editha sive Chronicon Vilodunense im Wiltshire Dialekt aus Ms. Cotton. Faustina B III

entities.description: Carl Horstmann's complete scholarly volume, including German introduction, a direct transcription of the Cotton witness, extensive critical and source notes, Latin appendices, remarks, and a second appendix. aje0623 represents the title page and poem on printed pp. 1-111; its PB placeholders explicitly omit printed pp. 112-116.
entities.label: Horstmann's 1883 edition of S. Editha
notes: Horstmann cites Black's 1830 edition as generally correct but repeatedly reports manuscript readings, corrections, erasures, and differences from Black, demonstrating direct recollation rather than derivation from Black alone.
Printed pp. 111-113 contain adjacent Latin material after the poem; only the poem portion of p. 111 is target content in aje0623.

rights.basis: The volume was published in 1883 and is outside the maximum United States copyright term; Internet Archive metadata marks the item NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT in the United States.
rights.component: 1883 edition, transcription, introduction, notes, and typography

access.accessMethod: No complete public digital copy was located. The University of Leeds Special Collections catalog reports an Archive Print copy at Large Modern History Y-3.1/WIL; consult Leeds for onsite access or reproduction possibilities.
access.entityLabel: Black's 1830 first edition of Chronicon Vilodunense

bibliographic.citation: William Henry Black, ed., Chronicon Vilodunense; sive de vita et miraculis Sanctae Edithae Regis Edgari filiae carmen vetus Anglicum, e codice unico Cottoniano (London: Typis Nicholsianis, 1830).
bibliographic.title: Chronicon Vilodunense; sive de vita et miraculis Sanctae Edithae Regis Edgari filiae carmen vetus Anglicum, e codice unico Cottoniano

entities.description: William Henry Black's first printed edition of the unique Cotton text, produced at the expense of Richard Colt Hoare. Horstmann describes it as generally correct and uses it as a collation point while returning to the manuscript.
entities.label: Black's 1830 first edition of Chronicon Vilodunense
notes: A current British Library component bibliography gives 1802, but the inspected Horstmann history, DIMEV, title citation, and library holdings support 1830.
Only 100 copies were reportedly printed; no complete public digital scan was located in the searches recorded here.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1830 and its nineteenth-century editor died long ago; the work is outside ordinary copyright terms. Repository or reproduction terms for a particular copy remain separate.
rights.component: 1830 edition and editorial matter
rights.jurisdiction: United States and jurisdictions with ordinary author-term protection

dateStatements.label: target component production
dateStatements.value: first quarter of the fifteenth century

dateStatements.label: probable copying date in modern scholarship

entities.description: The complete composite Cotton codex serving as the physical carrier for the sole witness of The Wilton Chronicle. The target poem is current ff. 199r-263r; adjacent Latin Wilton material follows on ff. 263r-264v, and the companion Middle English Life of St Æthelthryth follows on ff. 265r-279v. The target component was produced at Wilton in the first quarter of the fifteenth century.

historical_designations: Horstmann's foliation ff. 194-259 for the poem and appendices
holding.notes: The complete physical carrier is Cotton MS Faustina B III. Current foliation is five leaves higher than Horstmann's milestones across the target component; the poem is current ff. 199r-263r.

entities.label: London, British Library, Cotton MS Faustina B III, complete composite codex

physicalDescription.extent: Composite codex: i + 280 folios plus modern paper flyleaves; target poem ff. 199r-263r. The provider facsimile exposes front/back boards, endleaves and blanks, ff. 1r-280v, and spine.
notes: The poem has a twelve-folio gathering missing after conventional line 1030, between Horstmann old ff. 205 and 206 / current ff. 210 and 211.
Benskin treats the extant language as mixed and predominantly West Midland, produced by a scribe probably from northern Warwickshire or southern Staffordshire and likely working at Wilton.
The same hand copies the Edith poem, intervening Latin, and Æthelthryth poem; this does not establish autograph status.

rights.basis: The manuscript content is medieval and centuries outside copyright term. Physical ownership, photography, and provider terms are separate.
rights.component: Fifteenth-century manuscript textual and codicological content

rights.basis: The imaging service applies its own order terms and directs publication users to licensing. A paid image order does not by itself grant redistribution or publication rights.

next_steps: Ask University of Leeds Special Collections about photography or digitisation of Large Modern History Y-3.1/WIL.
Search Google Books, HathiTrust, British Library, Bodleian, Salisbury Cathedral, and WorldCat holdings by the full Latin title and imprint.
Cache the complete edition if a lawful direct download becomes available.
notes: The edition is extant and requestable through holdings; no-public-copy-found is only a digital-access finding.
openQuestions.question: Does a complete public digital facsimile of William Henry Black's scarce 1830 edition exist outside the repositories searched?

next_steps: Collate the raw file's 354 superscript and 9 italic HI1 spans against Horstmann and the manuscript.
Compare phase-3 additions separately; do not import its denser markup into aje0623.
notes: The available facsimile makes this research practical, but the present lineage does not equate every italic span with one kind of elision.
openQuestions.question: What exact source or editorial function does each of aje0623's 363 HI1 spans serve, and how do those spans relate to the later phase-3 artifact's 6,738 HI spans?

next_steps: Compare Dockray-Miller's full source apparatus with Horstmann's objections to identifying the named Legenda with the short Bollandist Vita.
Do not create a direct physical-exemplar relation without manuscript or edition evidence.
openQuestions.question: Which recension and physical exemplar of the Latin Edith legend underlies the Middle English poem?

next_steps: Ask licensing@bl.uk for an item-specific written determination, quoting the IIIF statement Public Domain in most countries other than the UK.
Keep the complete image bundle Git-ignored until redistribution rights are clear.
Separate use of public-domain medieval text and factual collation from republication of the image files.
openQuestions.question: What written reuse determination does the British Library apply to redistribution or publication of these IIIF images in the United Kingdom and in a commercial print-on-demand context?

next_steps: Use the parent record's current full-digital-coverage link and working IIIF manifest for access.
Retain the component's contrary sentence as historical catalog evidence until the British Library updates it.
notes: All 577 provider canvases were successfully cached and the ZIP passed integrity validation.
openQuestions.question: Are digital images of Cotton MS Faustina B III currently available despite the component catalog's legacy unavailability statement?

next_steps: Retry the advertised View Page routes in an interactive browser or through U-M-supported access.
Do not label the complete current image sequence available or unavailable until all sequences are checked.
notes: The current full text works, but automated page-view requests returned HTTP 403.
openQuestions.question: Can the complete historical set of 124 U-M-linked page images be retrieved and audited through the current interface?

reviewNotes: The abstract Wilton Chronicle, Horstmann edition/frame, complete physical Cotton carrier, raw AJE artifact, shared U-M item, later phase-3 artifact, and both complete facsimiles are separate entities.
The supported phase-3 version_of relation is directional, qualified, and never same_as; phase-3 access and rights are independently recorded.
Current BL foliation governs discovery; Horstmann legacy foliation remains attached to edition and raw milestone evidence.
Horstmann relates to Black by direct manuscript transcription plus collation, not by copying Black alone.
The complete BL bundle maps whole-to-whole to the complete physical composite carrier; target presence remains ff. 199r-263r / canvases 407-535.
The complete Horstmann volume is cached; target work_portion is only the poem on printed pp. 1-111 / PDF pp. 15-125.
All eight cached objects explicitly declare target_work_presence. Supporting catalogs, metadata, scholarship, and rate information are absent rather than given invented work portions.
U-M NoC-US is modeled as public_domain in the United States with the canonical RightsStatements URI; the raw 2006 generic warning is a separate dated rights record.
Repository XML/markup rights remain unknown because the root Unlicense is framed for software and has ambiguous corpus-data scope.
Latin appendix material remains outside aje0623 target content even where complete source objects preserve it.

lineage.summary: aje0623 is the raw 2006 University of Michigan Level-1, uncorrected-OCR representation of Horstmann's title page and Middle English poem on printed pp. 1-111. The repository artifact, shared U-M item, abstract Wilton Chronicle, Horstmann edition and frame, sole physical Cotton witness, later proofed/restructured/expanded phase-3 CME00147 artifact, and complete edition and manuscript facsimiles remain distinct. Empty page placeholders for printed pp. 112-116 do not import the Latin appendices into aje0623 content.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Wilton poet
catalogSummary.date: Around 1420; sole surviving witness from the first quarter of the fifteenth century; first published in 1830
catalogSummary.region: Wilton, Wiltshire, England; an inferred South Wiltshire original is transmitted in a mixed-language copy made by a scribe associated with the West Midlands
catalogSummary.title: The Wilton Chronicle

catalogingSubject.label: The Wilton Chronicle
catalogingSubject.scope_note: One anonymous Middle English verse work composed for or in close association with the Benedictine convent at Wilton around 1420. It joins Wilton's institutional and royal history to the life, relic translation, miracles, and cult of St Edith. The raw aje0623 XML represents Horstmann's title page and the poem on printed pp. 1-111, with empty page placeholders for omitted preliminaries and printed pp. 112-116. No Latin appendix content is encoded or counted as part of aje0623.

contentParts.label: The Wilton Chronicle

locators.value: 407-535; target ends above the adjacent Latin opening on shared canvas 535

notes: Five raw L rows are non-verbal display or lacuna rows; 4,983 L elements contain extant verbal verse and conventional lineation has 4,986 positions.
The poem and adjacent Latin share current f. 263r / IIIF canvas 535; the verse ends above the Latin opening.

assertion.notes: Around 1420 is a scholarly inference supported by the complete Horstmann edition, the manuscript context, and modern scholarship. The adjacent Latin founders list used in that dating argument is not encoded as content in aje0623.

dateStatements.display: Around 1420, probably during the reign of Henry V (1413-1422)

scope.description: Composition or final compilation of the Middle English Wilton Chronicle.

dateStatements.display: First quarter of the fifteenth century (sole surviving witness)
notes: The date belongs to the manuscript component and supports, but does not independently fix, composition around 1420.

scope.description: Date assigned to the Cotton Faustina B III component containing the work.
First printed edition identified by Horstmann and DIMEV.

extent.description: Raw aje0623 structural axis: one BODY/DIV1 target work containing 1,245 LG and 4,988 L elements. Five L rows at raw positions 814, 1031, 1032, 1366, and 3520 are non-verbal lacuna/display rows. The remaining 4,983 L elements contain extant verbal verse. Three display rows stand for missing conventional positions and two are extra markers for the twelve-folio lacuna, so Horstmann's conventional running lineation has 4,986 positions. The title page and omitted front/back page placeholders are zero-count representation parts.

formStatements.description: One Middle English narrative poem represented by 1,245 raw XML LG elements and 4,988 raw L elements; five L rows are non-verbal display or lacuna rows, 4,983 contain extant verbal verse, and conventional lineation has 4,986 positions.

scope.description: The abstract Wilton Chronicle.
Life, cult, relic translation, visions, and miracles of St Edith of Wilton.
A vernacular life and miracle collection centered on a female royal saint.

assertion.notes: The label describes one major function and should not displace the work's hagiographic center.

scope.description: Chronologically organized history of Wilton, Wessex, royal founders, and the saint's cult integrated into the verse life.
Vernacular devotional and institutional verse intended for a religious community and its wider audience.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The extant copy has mixed dialect features. Benskin separates the likely South Wiltshire originals from a copyist associated linguistically with northern Warwickshire or southern Staffordshire and probably working at Wilton.
Short Latin quotations occur inside the Middle English poem, while German headings and notes belong to Horstmann's editorial frame; neither creates a Latin appendix part in aje0623.

scope.description: The language of the Middle English verse work represented by 4,983 extant verbal verse rows within 4,988 raw L elements.

notes: The U-M catalog places St Edith in an author position, but she is the poem subject; Goscelin is a possible source-author association, not the anonymous Middle English author.
The preferred title follows current scholarship. Chronicon Vilodunense and The Life of St Edith remain searchable alternates.
The target is one verse work in one witness. Raw LG groups are XML stanza containers, not separately cataloged works.
The title page, German apparatus, and omitted-page placeholders are zero-count representation parts.
The Latin appendices on printed pp. 111-116 remain edition/manuscript context only and are completely outside aje0623 target content and arithmetic.
The raw structural counts, verbal-line count, and conventional line positions are deliberately reported separately.
The surviving manuscript copy is not treated as autograph. Benskin's analysis separates original language, scribal language, place of composition, and place of copying.
Current foliation governs discovery and facsimile navigation; Horstmann's old foliation and the raw XML's corrupt milestone sequence remain representation evidence.
The complete British Library IIIF bundle and complete Horstmann volume are cached; work_portion locators associate the target without cropping the source objects.
The phase-3 CME00147 artifact is a later proofed, restructured, and expanded repository expression related by supported version_of, never same_as.

next_steps: Retain anonymous authorship unless documentary or paleographic evidence identifies a person.
Consult the full argument and notes in Dockray-Miller and later scholarship for any post-2009 reassessment of gender or office.
notes: Close institutional affiliation is well supported; personal identity and profession are not.
openQuestions.question: Was the anonymous Wilton poet a chaplain, another male cleric, a nun, or another member of the abbey's textual community?

next_steps: Compare Dockray-Miller's source notes against Horstmann's objections and current Goscelin scholarship.
Do not convert the Auctoritates list into a claim for an identified physical exemplar.
openQuestions.question: Which recension or physical exemplar of the Legenda sanctae Edithae underlies the poem, and how exactly is it related to the short Goscelin-associated Vita printed by the Bollandists?

next_steps: Compare source analogues and later summaries without presenting reconstruction as surviving text.
Seek physical or codicological evidence for when the gathering was removed.
notes: Horstmann's approximately 980 missing lines is an estimate and is excluded from the surviving verse count.
openQuestions.question: What was the exact text and line count of the twelve-folio gathering lost after surviving line 1030?

next_steps: Collate aje0623's 354 superscript and 9 italic HI1 spans against Horstmann's page images and the manuscript.
Compare the later phase-3 revision without importing its denser markup or Latin content into aje0623.
notes: The raw OCR/Level-1 file and later proofed phase-3 expression encode typography at materially different granularities.
openQuestions.question: What exact source or editorial function does each of aje0623's 363 HI1 spans serve, and how should they be compared with the later phase-3 file's 6,738 HI spans?

next_steps: Retry the current View Page routes in an interactive browser or through U-M-supported access.
Do not describe the linked image set as currently complete or unavailable until all sequences are checked.
notes: The current full-text page is public, but automated page-view requests returned HTTP 403 during this audit.

assertion.notes: Composition at or for Wilton is strongly supported; the author's office, gender, and personal identity remain unknown.

scope.description: Likely place and institutional setting of composition.

assertion.notes: The extant manuscript language is mixed and predominantly West Midland; that scribal profile must not be flattened into the work's composition place.

scope.description: Benskin's reconstruction of the likely dialect of the work's originals.
Broad cultural origin and historical horizon.

assertion.notes: The author had close knowledge of Wilton and probably wrote for its female religious community, but no personal name or secure clerical status is known.

notes: Older suggestions that the poet was one of Wilton's chaplains or that the sole copy was autograph remain hypotheses, not established authorship.
Benskin distinguishes authorial language from the extant copyist's mixed dialect and rejects a simple autograph model.

scope.description: The Middle English verse work.
Compilation and adaptation of hagiographic, historical, institutional, and local source material.
The modern title page, headings, transcription, punctuation, typography, and German notes represented in aje0623; omitted-page placeholders are separately recorded as representation parts.
Edith's life, sanctity, death, relics, posthumous appearances, miracles, and patronage of Wilton.

subjects.label: Wilton Abbey, its founders, and convent identity

scope.description: Institutional origins, benefactors, property, memory, devotional obligations, and historical identity of Wilton's female religious community.
Edith, Wulfthryth, Wilton's nuns, abbesses, visions, and the construction of female religious history.
Healing, punishment, visions, protection, and posthumous intercession.

subjects.label: Translation of relics and shrine devotion

scope.description: Edith's incorrupt body, elevation, shrine, relic protection, and later cult.
Royal succession, West Saxon history, Norman rule, and Wilton's place in English history.
The abstract Middle English work.

summaries.value: The Wilton Chronicle is an anonymous Middle English poem composed around 1420 for or in close association with Wilton Abbey. It frames the life and miracles of St Edith within an account of Wessex, Wilton's foundation, royal patrons, and institutional memory. After Edith's life and death, the poem narrates the translation of her incorrupt body, shrine and relic miracles, episodes concerning her mother Wulfthryth, and later interventions protecting Wilton and its female religious community. Its joined hagiography and history constructs a usable past for the abbey rather than functioning as a purely secular chronicle.

scope.description: The single surviving Middle English verse work represented by aje0623.

summaries.value: The poem opens with English and West Saxon history and Wilton's founders, moves into Edgar's encounter with Wulfthryth, and then marks a twelve-folio manuscript loss after conventional line 1030. The surviving text resumes with Edith's upbringing, ascetic life, miracles, death, and first shrine cult. Horstmann later marks a second part beginning with the translation of Edith's relics; the remainder narrates royal patrons, punishments, healings, visions, and Edith's continuing protection of Wilton. The raw XML ends with the poem on printed p. 111. Its printed pp. 112-116 DIV1 is explicitly omitted back matter and contains only PB placeholders, so the adjacent Latin appendices are outside aje0623 content.

scope.description: Cataloging boundary among abstract work, edition frame, physical witness, encoding, and adjacent content.

summaries.value: The raw header's LANGUAGE value ger reflects Horstmann's German editorial framing, not the target work's language. The header also places St Edith and Goscelin in author fields, but the medieval poem is anonymous and Edith is its subject. Current British Library foliation is five leaves later than Horstmann's legacy milestones: the poem is now ff. 199r-263r rather than ff. 194r-258r. Current f. 263r / IIIF canvas 535 contains the poem ending above and adjacent Latin below; only the poem portion belongs to aje0623.

assertion.notes: Current scholarship uses this concise title while recognizing that the work is both institutional history and hagiography.

scope.description: The abstract Middle English work.

notes: Horstmann argued that Edith's legend, rather than annalistic history alone, is the poem's central purpose.

scope.description: Descriptive title emphasizing the work's hagiographic center.

notes: This title should not imply that the text is merely a chronicle; its hagiographic and institutional-historical functions are inseparable.

scope.description: Historical title used by Leland, Black, and later scholarship.
First surviving verse line, with Horstmann's punctuation omitted.

titles.value: Almyȝty god in trinite, fader & sone and holy goste

scope.description: Final surviving verse line and closer.

titles.value: To þe same plase þer-as seynt Ede ys. Amen.

work.summary: The Wilton Chronicle is an anonymous Middle English poem composed around 1420 for or in close association with Wilton Abbey. It frames the life and miracles of St Edith within an account of Wessex, Wilton's foundation, royal patrons, and institutional memory. After Edith's life and death, the poem narrates the translation of her incorrupt body, shrine and relic miracles, episodes concerning her mother Wulfthryth, and later interventions protecting Wilton and its female religious community. Its joined hagiography and history constructs a usable past for the abbey rather than functioning as a purely secular chronicle.
